content.assessment: From an implementation-planning perspective, T1 uses an existing administrative lever (the centrally graded national exam and the regulated interview cap) rather than requiring new legal architecture, which improves near-term deliverability. However, the sequencing logic is incomplete: it targets the admissions instrument while leaving upstream non-application and geographic-access gaps unaddressed, meaning the mechanism cannot reach the populations most affected by sorting even if perfectly executed. No monitoring or adaptation loop is specified to detect coaching-market displacement to new exam formats, and the disputed statistics baseline elsewhere in the evidence base raises doubt about whether effects could be credibly monitored at all.
opportunities: Could be piloted at a subset of schools/exam administrations before national rollout, since the exam is already centrally graded
Interview-weight reallocation could be tested incrementally within the existing 25% cap without new legislation
strengths: Reform channel (exam redesign, interview weight reallocation) is administratively existing and centrally controlled, lowering legal/actor-capacity barriers to initiating a pilot
Interview-weight cap already in regulation gives a bounded, low-risk lever to test incrementally
threats: SES-index proxies are contestable and could be captured or delegitimized by politically connected advantaged families, undermining monitoring integrity
Absence of primary-school sorting reform means measured gains at the admissions gate could be offset by unmeasured upstream sorting, confounding evaluation
weaknesses: No mechanism to reach disadvantaged elementary schools that do not facilitate competition participation, so the intervention may not touch the non-applicant pool at all
Geographic clustering of 6/8-year schools in urban areas is not addressed by an admissions-criteria fix, leaving a structural access gap unmitigated
No specified feedback loop to detect whether coaching investment displaces to new exam formats rather than being reduced
